summary: csvCell applies RFC 4180 quoting but never neutralizes formula-leading cells (=, +, -, @), so CSV export is vulnerable to spreadsheet formula injection via todo titles/descriptions.
why: The mechanical observation is accurate — csvCell (export-data.ts:50-52) applies only RFC 4180 quoting and formula-leading cells pass through — but the defect claim is refuted on three grounds. (1) Threat model: buildCsv(user.id) exports only the authenticated user's own todos; Lifeline has no sharing/collaboration, so no attacker-controlled data can enter another user's export. The claimed scenario is the account owner injecting a formula into their own account and exporting it — no trust boundary is crossed, which is why security teams (Google, Microsoft) classify self-export CSV injection as a client-side spreadsheet concern, and modern Excel/LibreOffice prompt before executing DDE/external content. (2) The cited requirement is fabricated: grepping all planning and discovery docs for 'formula', 'injection', 'leading =', 'neutraliz', 'HYPERLINK' finds no 'hunt spec' mandating leading-= escaping anywhere in the repo contracts. (3) The behavior is documented intentional parity: audit-domain-logic.md:81-83 (ground truth from the old app) specifies the exact CSV format with no formula escaping, 02-api-contract-v1.md:46 requires the 'same payload family as old', and export-data.test.ts:157-177 locks the quoting-only format. Moreover, the CSV is round-trippable via import (.csv accepted per audit-web-client.md:71 and feature-inventory line 35), so prepending neutralization characters would corrupt titles on re-import — a real data-integrity regression in an actual flow, traded for a hypothetical self-attack. Not a reportable defect under the review's 'concrete failure scenario, no speculation' bar.

summary: Account reset issues three independent auto-commit deletes (todos, custom tags, settings) with no transaction, so a failure after the first delete leaves a half-reset account.
why: The factual observation is accurate: ResetAccount.execute (codebase/apps/server/src/application/data-transfer/reset-account.ts:20-22) issues three sequential repo calls, and the Drizzle implementations (todo/tag/settings repositories in src/infrastructure/repositories) each run a single auto-commit DELETE with no shared transaction or unit-of-work. However, as a defect claim it does not survive against the contracts and ground truth. (1) This is a faithful parity port of the old app: audit-domain-logic.md §10 describes the old POST /api/reset-account (index.js:138-151) as three hard-deletes with no transaction mentioned — the same audit explicitly calls out transactions where they existed (import-replace 'single transaction') and where they were missing as a bug ('Loop not transactional — partial imports possible'), and it flags no atomicity problem for reset. (2) The planning docs deliberately enumerated which atomicity gaps the rebuild would fix: 05-decisions.md fix #10 upgrades IMPORT to one transaction; reset is not on the fix list. The API contract (02-api-contract-v1.md) annotates POST /api/v1/import as 'transactional' but POST /api/v1/account/reset carries no such requirement — the asymmetry is intentional, not an oversight. (3) The failure impact is self-healing, as the reviewer concedes: each delete is idempotent and user-scoped, ordering deletes todos (the primary data) first, and a retry of the same endpoint converges to the fully-reset state. There is no data corruption, no cross-user leakage, no permanently wedged state — the transient intermediate state is 'some data the user asked to delete still exists after a 500', which the 500 itself signals. The existing test (reset-account.test.ts) covers the contract-specified behavior (what is deleted vs kept). Wrapping it in a transaction would be a hardening improvement, but under this review's rubric it is not a demonstrable defect against the documented contract or old-app behavior — the docs treat reset's semantics as parity, and only import was promised atomicity.

summary: MCP_API_KEY_PEPPER non-empty requirement is skipped whenever AUTH_DISABLED='1', so the actual production deployment (compose defaults AUTH_DISABLED:-1 per audit-deploy-ops.md) silently runs with pepper='' — reintroducing the exact bug decision #6 in planning/05-decisions.md says is fixed ('env validation now requires it in production').
why:
The code fact is accurate — env.ts:119-125 only enforces non-empty MCP_API_KEY_PEPPER when `isProduction && !authDisabled` — but the claim's failure scenario and "reintroduced bug" framing do not hold up.

1. The deployment premise is wrong. The reviewer cites audit-deploy-ops.md for "compose defaults AUTH_DISABLED:-1", but that default belongs to the OLD app's LOCAL compose.yaml (audit-deploy-ops.md, "lifeline-app env: ... AUTH_DISABLED:-1"). The actual VPS production deployment, compose.production.yaml, explicitly "defaults flip to prod: ... AUTH_DISABLED:-0 ... + env MCP_INTERNAL_SHARED_SECRET, MCP_API_KEY_PEPPER" and compose.production.env.example sets AUTH_DISABLED=0. So the documented production deployment DOES go through the pepper validation, which is exactly what decision #6 promised. The rebuilt codebase itself ships no production compose at all yet (only compose.dev.yaml, a dev Postgres; the deploy-swap phase is pending), so there is no rebuilt artifact that boots production with AUTH_DISABLED=1.

2. The carve-out is intentional and tested, not a silent regression. The env.ts module docstring (lines 9-11) states the rule explicitly: "In production (unless AUTH_DISABLED='1') AUTH0_DOMAIN + AUTH0_AUDIENCE and a non-empty MCP_API_KEY_PEPPER are required". env.test.ts:58-67 has a dedicated test, "AUTH_DISABLED=1 relaxes the production auth requirements (but not DATABASE_URL)". The carve-out is structurally required: the old local compose runs NODE_ENV=production with AUTH_DISABLED=1 and empty AUTH0_DOMAIN/AUDIENCE (and no pepper), and 05-decisions.md's "Env compatibility (VPS drop-in)" section requires honoring that configuration — an unconditional pepper requirement would make the documented local-compose configuration unbootable. Decision #6's one-liner ("env validation now requires it in production") is shorthand for the same production path where the parallel Auth0 requirements apply, and those unambiguously carry the AUTH_DISABLED exemption (feature inventory line 10 calls AUTH_DISABLED a "local-dev bypass"; guest mode is documented server-side as "works only locally").

3. The security impact is overstated even in the misuse scenario. MCP key secrets are 24 random bytes base64url (~192-bit entropy, mcp-key-crypto.ts:26), stored as HMAC-SHA256(secret, pepper). With pepper='' a leaked hash still cannot be inverted or brute-forced against a 192-bit random secret; the pepper is defense-in-depth, not the sole DB-leak defense. And this matches the old backend's behavior byte-for-byte in the same local-compose mode, so nothing regressed relative to the ground truth for that configuration.

A residual quibble — one could wish for a boot-time WARN log when production+AUTH_DISABLED runs with an empty pepper — is a hardening suggestion, not a defect anchored to a real deployment flow.

summary: AuthState.warmUp runs exactly once at boot and is never retried, so a transient JWKS/discovery fetch failure leaves /health/ready returning 503 for the lifetime of the process even though token verification works fine (jose fetches the JWKS lazily per request).
why: Confirmed, not refuted. warmUp is invoked exactly once, fire-and-forget, at main.ts:24; no retry, interval, or re-warm exists anywhere in apps/server. jwksWarmedUp (auth.ts:233) is only set true inside warmUp's success path (auth.ts:264); on a transient discovery/JWKS fetch failure the catch (auth.ts:267-270) sets lastError and the flag stays false for the process lifetime. getReadiness (auth.ts:285) computes ready = configured && jwksWarmedUp, and health.ts:28-30 turns that into a permanent 503 on public GET /health/ready, echoing lastError verbatim in the JSON. The request-path verifier (createRemoteJWKSet at auth.ts:57) is completely independent of warmUp's plain fetch calls and lazily fetches/caches the JWKS per jose semantics, so token verification recovers the moment Auth0/DNS recovers while readiness never does — the claimed divergence is real and reachable. No test covers warm-up failure/recovery (only a DB-down 503 test in app.test.ts:35), and the contract docs (02-api-contract-v1.md:25, 05-decisions.md:70) document that readiness reflects JWKS warm state but nowhere sanction one-shot-never-retry latching. Mitigating factors: requires the transient outage to hit the boot window, a process restart re-runs warmUp, /health/live still returns 200 (so k8s would not restart, it would just never admit the pod), and AUTH_DISABLED/compose mode bypasses it entirely. Not data loss or a security hole (lastError is an infra error string), so medium: wrong behavior in a real operational flow — an instance with fully functional auth is permanently excluded from traffic after a boot-time blip.

summary: Guest-mode stats compute avgDuration as timeSpentTotal / totalTodos (including zero-duration todos), while the server contract computes it as the rounded mean of POSITIVE durations only (statsResponseSchema doc comment; get-stats.ts:42-48), so the same data shows a different average in guest vs server mode.
why: The computational divergence the reviewer describes is real in the narrow sense — guest mode (stats-lib.ts:106) divides timeSpentTotal by ALL in-range todos while the server (get-stats.ts:42-48) averages positive durations only — but it is not a defect in the rebuild; it is deliberate, test-pinned parity with the old app's ground truth. The old app's guest branch (C:/Users/ziyad/Lifeline/client/src/components/statistics/Statistics.jsx:151-153, and again at :192-194) computes exactly `avgDuration = totalTodos ? Math.round(timeSpentTotal / totalTodos) : 0` over all guest todos including zero durations, while the old server computed mean of >0 durations (audit-domain-logic.md:74). So the guest-vs-server discrepancy existed identically in the old app, and the clean-room rebuild's mandate is fidelity to that ground truth. The rebuild makes this intent explicit: stats-lib.ts's header comment says it is 'the old Statistics.jsx ... guest local computation, extracted pure', and stats-lib.test.ts:67-83 deliberately includes a duration:0 todo (line 71) and asserts avgDuration: 30 '// round(90 / 3)' — i.e., a test already covers and pins the exact zero-inclusive behavior the reviewer flags as wrong. The reviewer's cross-mode scenario is also weak as a 'real flow': per planning/05-decisions.md:47-48, guest localStorage data is wiped at login with no upgrade path (documented product behavior), so 'the same data' does not migrate from guest to server automatically. The only kernel of truth is a doc-comment inconsistency: packages/shared/src/stats.ts:21 says avgDuration is the 'Mean of positive durations', which describes the server producer, while the guest producer of the same StatsResponse type uses the old client's zero-inclusive formula. That is a stale/over-specific comment on a shared type, not wrong behavior — a style/doc nit the review instructions exclude. Refuted.

summary: Composer's dueDate fallback rejects the 'today'/'tomorrow' day tokens, so tasks added from the default Today view (and /day/tomorrow) are created with dueDate null and immediately disappear from the day view.
why: The claimed defect does not exist in the current code. The claim asserts that Composer's dueDate fallback uses a /^\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}$/ regex that rejects 'today'/'tomorrow' tokens, sending dueDate=null. In reality, apps/web/src/features/todos/components/Composer.tsx line 173 reads `const dueDate = scheduleDate || resolveDayString(effectiveDate);`, and resolveDayString (apps/web/src/features/todos/lib/day-filter.ts:32-36) explicitly converts 'today' to format(now,'yyyy-MM-dd') and 'tomorrow' to the next day's date. So a task added from the default Today view is created with today's concrete date, matches matchesDay, and renders on the Today view. Moreover, a dedicated regression test exists for this exact scenario: apps/web/src/features/todos/components/composer.test.tsx lines 57-71 render the Composer with effectiveDate='today', submit a title-only task, and assert the createTodo payload dueDate equals today's YYYY-MM-DD date — the test comment even notes this token-fallthrough bug 'used to' exist and was fixed. The reviewer appears to have reviewed a stale version of the file. The secondary sub-claim about RecurrenceSelector baseDate (line 488) is technically accurate that the regex fallback yields '' for tokens, but baseDate is only used to PRE-FILL the startDate input in the recurrence modal (RecurrenceSelector.tsx:94); the user still sees an empty start-date field and Apply validates the range, so the consequence is at most a missing default value in an optional modal — not the claimed data-loss/vanishing-task defect, and not the defect as stated. The core failure scenario (task created with dueDate null and disappearing from Today) is not reachable.
